cataloging_subject.label: York Corpus Christi Play
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous civic biblical dramatic cycle represented by the pinned U-M/OTA-derived York XML: fifty separately encoded pageant divisions drawn through Richard Beadle's 1982 edition. It is one historically assembled cycle or compilation, not fifty unrelated works. The fifty divisions preserve the 1-47 sequence plus parallel Cardmakers play 3B, the two-line Vintners' Cana remnant 22A, and later Coronation fragment 46A; they omit the textless Ironmongers entry and the unregistered Funeral of the Virgin. The medieval work, physical witnesses, Beadle's edition, the electronic encoding, and this repository artifact remain distinct transmission layers.

catalog_summary.title: York Corpus Christi Play
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous York playwrights, revisers, and civic compilers
catalog_summary.editor: Richard Beadle (1982 immediate source edition); U-M/OTA electronic edition (1993)
catalog_summary.date: Developed from at least the late fourteenth century and revised through the sixteenth century; final medieval performance in 1569

notes: Established discovery title for the historical civic dramatic compilation; the exact electronic boundary is recorded in the cataloging scope and alternate titles rather than conflated with this preferred intellectual title.
Exact title in the electronic header and Beadle's 1982 source edition.

titles.value: The York Plays (fifty-division U-M/OTA electronic representation)
notes: Boundary label for the represented source selection, not a replacement title for the historical work.

agents.name: Anonymous York playwrights, revisers, and civic compilers
notes: The pageants are the work of multiple unknown hands across a long civic transmission; guild performance responsibility is not converted into authorship.

scope.description: The cycle's medieval dramatic texts and revisions, without assigning individual guilds as authors
Historical assembly and continuing revision of the civic pageant sequence
The 1982 immediate source edition, not medieval authorship
The electronic text as a whole; Kinneavy's corrections affect selected passages whose exact locations are not itemized in the XML header

date_statements.display: The pageant tradition developed over the late fourteenth and fifteenth centuries; individual pageant dates and stages of revision vary

scope.description: Broad development window, not a claim of simultaneous composition

notes: The inherited XML value 1440 is an imprecise legacy collection-level date and is not normalized as a single composition year.

date_statements.display: York civic records document pageant wagons in 1376/1377

scope.description: Earliest documentary evidence for the civic performance tradition, not attestation of every surviving text

date_statements.display: The civic pageant tradition and constituent texts continued to be revised through the sixteenth century; the final medieval performance was in 1569

notes: York is the civic performance, record-production, and compilation context; the statement does not prove one exact composition site for every pageant.

scope.description: The civic Register and pageant sequence

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Brief Latin incipits, rubrics, and formulae are embedded rather than a sustained secondary-language component.

form_statements.description: Dramatic verse with speech labels, rubrics, stage directions, and limited embedded prose-like matter

assertion.notes: The 13,638 XML L elements are a representation metric, not a clean intellectual line count: numbering is partial and Play XVI includes shared and alternative Goldsmiths/Masons blocks.

genre_statements.label: Cycle drama
notes: Cycle is used as a discovery term without implying a single authorial Urtext or an unchanging complete sequence.

subject_statements.label: Passion, death, and resurrection of Jesus

summaries.value: A civic Middle English dramatic sequence spanning the Fall of the Angels, Creation and Old Testament history, Christ's birth, ministry, Passion and Resurrection, Marian conclusion, and Last Judgment. York crafts maintained and performed pageants on wagons at established stations; the surviving Register records a layered, revised sequence rather than a pristine authorial cycle.

The XML contains fifty top-level DIV1 pageants: the numbered 1-47 sequence, parallel play 3B, a two-line 22A remnant, and 46A's later Coronation fragment. It excludes the Ironmongers' textless register space and the Funeral of the Virgin, which was never entered in the Register. Play XVI serializes shared and alternative Masons/Goldsmiths blocks as 539 L elements and must not be treated as a simple 539-line intellectual play.

The XML has 13,638 L, 2,754 SP, 2,754 SPEAKER, 367 PB, and 368 EPB elements. Only 2,815 L elements carry N values. Direct comparison with the four OTA text files shows exactly 13,638 text rows after excluding control records, with 13,226 normalized exact row matches; this supports direct derivation but also exposes formatting, correction, and Play XVI ordering changes.

extent.description: One cycle-level compilation represented by fifty top-level XML containers and fifty-one counted pageant or fragment components. Forty-nine top-level containers count once; Play XVI is a zero-count editorial container with two nested counted pageant units, the Masons' Herod and Goldsmiths' Magi. The count retains the two-line 22A and 47-line 46A fragments as explicit components. The XML has 13,638 L elements, but no clean total of intellectual verse lines is asserted because numbering is incomplete, lacunae are represented as line elements, and Play XVI serializes shared text plus alternative Masons and Goldsmiths branches.

content_parts.label: XVI. The Masons / The Goldsmiths — Herod / The Magi
content_parts.summary: Beadle's editorial container combines two separately registered pageants whose middle Herod-and-kings scene exists in Masons and Goldsmiths versions. It is not counted independently from its two nested pageant units.
notes: The XML has 539 L elements assembled from shared Yk.16 rows and distinct Yk.16G/Yk.16M variant blocks. Because those branches repeat alternative versions of the same middle scene, 539 is not asserted as a clean intellectual line count.

content_parts.label: The Masons — Herod
content_parts.summary: The separately registered Masons' pageant: Herod's boastful opening plus the Masons version of his encounter with the three kings.

locators.value: Yk.16 shared lines 1-56 plus Yk.16M alternative lines 129-261

notes: The XML does not wrap this version as a child element; the locator reconstructs the branch from preserved OTA record labels and the manuscript folio boundary.

content_parts.label: The Goldsmiths — The Magi
content_parts.summary: The separately registered Goldsmiths' pageant: the kings' approach, the Goldsmiths version of their encounter with Herod, the Adoration, and their departure by another route.

locators.value: Yk.16 shared lines 57-128 and 273-389 plus Yk.16G alternative lines 129-261

notes: The XML does not wrap this version as a child element; the locator reconstructs the branch from preserved OTA record labels and the manuscript folio boundary.

related_works.title: The Towneley Plays
notes: The Towneley manuscript preserves adapted or borrowed material related to selected York pageants; it is a distinct compilation and not a witness of the exact York cycle as a whole.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source XML, CME/source/CME_from_OTA/York.xml, pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Direct source for the encoded text, header, fifty DIV1 identifiers, and repository boundary.

evidence.citation: Targeted XML and OTA source-file inspection performed 2026-07-15.
evidence.summary: Counted 50 DIV1, 13,638 L, 2,754 SP/SPEAKER, 367 PB, and 368 EPB elements; inspected N sequence, lacuna markers, page boundaries, and one-for-one OTA source rows. Play XVI uses Yk.16/Yk.16G/Yk.16M shared and alternative blocks.

evidence.citation: The York Plays, ed. Richard Beadle (London: Edward Arnold, 1982), 537 pp.
evidence.summary: Immediate source edition. Cached title, copyright, and contents preview pages confirm bibliographic data, fifty encoded pageant headings, guilds, and printed-page boundaries.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive, York miracle play cycle, ota:1392 / U-1392-B.
evidence.summary: Current OTA item metadata, identifier, issue date, file inventory, and Academic Use designation.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive U-1392-B plain-text files york1-york4 and input conventions.
evidence.summary: Five cached public files preserve plays 1-18, 19-30, 31-40, 41-47, and encoding conventions; direct comparison establishes the XML derivation and Play XVI variant blocks.

evidence.citation: British Library, Add MS 35290, York Mystery Plays, current Archives and Manuscripts record 040-002088765.
evidence.summary: Current shelfmark, dates, physical description, provenance, detailed pageant folio map, access restrictions, and current lack of digital images.

evidence.citation: Clifford Davidson, ed., The York Corpus Christi Play, Middle English Texts Series reading edition (2011; current web edition).
evidence.summary: Current scholarly account of the civic cycle, pageant wagons and stations, Ordo layers, Register production and revisions, guild-copy relationships, omitted entries, and performance through 1569.

evidence.citation: Records of Early English Drama, York collection, pinned repository commit 1bec27e917f17f9ebdefc6ec6f9c79c8a1605f66.
evidence.summary: Documentary evidence for the York civic performance tradition and the Ordo; cached exact TEI source is separately modeled in lineage.

evidence.citation: Peter Meredith, ‘The Ordo Paginarum and the Development of the York Tilemakers' Pageant,’ Leeds Studies in English, n.s. 11 (1980): 59-73.
evidence.summary: Shows that the Ordo is layered, erased, and rewritten and that the Tilemakers entry cannot be treated as a homogeneous 1415 record.

evidence.citation: A. C. Cawley, ‘The Sykes MS of the York Scriveners' Play,’ Leeds Studies in English 7-8 (1952): 45-80.
evidence.summary: Description, photograph, complete transcription, and collation of the separate Scriveners witness against the Register; neither manuscript is a direct copy of the other.

evidence.citation: York Plays, ed. Lucy Toulmin Smith (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1885).
evidence.summary: The first complete edition prints the Masons as Play XVI and the Goldsmiths as Play XVII. Smith explains that their middle scene overlaps, with a Masons-specific opening and Goldsmiths-specific approach and Adoration, supporting two nested pageant units inside Beadle's later combined Play XVI container.

evidence.citation: Gerald Byron Kinneavy, A Concordance to the York Plays (New York: Garland, 1986), xxxviii + 936 pp., ISBN 0-8240-8656-2.
evidence.summary: The electronic header identifies corrections recorded on pp. xxxi-xxxii as incorporated into the electronic text; no complete public digital copy was found.

open_questions.question: What exact textual genealogy and editorial rationale underlie the Yk.16, Yk.16G, and Yk.16M shared and alternative blocks serialized inside Play XVI?
next_steps: Consult the complete 1982 edition and Kinneavy's correction list, then collate the shared and variant blocks without treating alternative branches as additive intellectual lines.
notes: The division is securely labeled XVI, Masons/Goldsmiths, Herod/Magi; the unresolved issue is internal branch genealogy, not play numbering.

open_questions.question: What is the current authoritative shelfmark and custody statement for the A/Y Memorandum Book containing the Ordo Paginarum?
next_steps: Obtain a current Borthwick Institute or York City Archives catalog response; preserve the conflict between current REED attribution and older scholarship until then.

open_questions.question: What recoverable evidence remains for the lost pageant copies supplied by individual York crafts to the Register compilers?
next_steps: Continue documentary and archival research without inventing one manuscript entity per guild; treat the Sykes Scriveners copy as the only separately surviving script currently established.

notes: Guild names describe recorded production responsibility, not proven authorship.
The Funeral of the Virgin is not folded into XML Play XLIV: the encoded Death of the Virgin is a 196-line pageant, while the Funeral was never entered in the Register and is absent.
The related Towneley material is collateral adaptation or borrowing, not the exact York target work.
